sources.title: Recurve Archery - Back Tension - The Release

entries.description: The draw shoulder elevates towards the ear instead of staying low and engaged, preventing proper back tension and creating instability. (Estimated ranges)
root_causes: Trying to muscle the draw with arm
observable_signs: Draw shoulder height significantly increases from setup to anchor
Draw shoulder appears higher than bow shoulder at full draw
entries.score_impact: Leads to inconsistent anchor, poor power transfer, and vertical string blur, causing inconsistent arrow placement.
entries.embed_text: draw anchor draw_shoulder spine draw_shoulder_height_diff 20-50mm draw shoulder shrug lack back tension muscle draw shoulder down back relax neck inconsistent anchor poor power transfer vertical string blur inconsistent arrow placement

entries.description: The draw elbow angle becomes too acute at full draw, indicating the archer is primarily using their bicep and forearm muscles rather than engaging the back muscles effectively. (estimated ranges)
observable_signs: draw_elbow_angle < 90 degrees at full draw
entries.score_impact: Leads to inconsistent draw length, poor release, and horizontal grouping errors due to variable string pressure.
entries.embed_text: draw anchor aim draw_elbow draw_shoulder draw_elbow_angle 45-89 degrees Draw Elbow Collapse open elbow point elbow back feel shoulder blade inconsistent draw poor release horizontal grouping

entries.description: The bow arm elbow is locked straight or hyperextended, creating tension and potential for string slap, and reducing the arm's ability to absorb shock. (estimated ranges)
observable_signs: bow_arm_extension_angle > 178 degrees (elbow locked)
coaching_cues: Push through the heel of your hand
entries.score_impact: Can cause string slap on the forearm, inconsistent bow hand pressure, and poor shock absorption leading to inconsistent arrow flight.
entries.embed_text: setup draw anchor aim bow_elbow bow_arm_extension_angle 179-185 degrees Bow Arm Hyperextension soft elbow micro-bend push heel hand string slap inconsistent bow hand

entries.description: The archer leans excessively forward from the waist, compromising balance and stability, and making it harder to engage core and back muscles correctly. (estimated ranges)
observable_signs: spine_forward_lean > 10 degrees from vertical
entries.score_impact: Leads to balance issues, inconsistent body alignment, and can cause vertical grouping errors due to unstable platform.
entries.embed_text: setup draw anchor aim spine hip spine_forward_lean 11-45 degrees Excessive Spine Forward Lean stand tall stack spine engage core balance issues vertical grouping errors

entries.description: The draw wrist is either flexed or extended, causing inconsistent string contact and potential for string torque, leading to poor arrow flight. (observable sign)
observable_signs: Draw wrist visibly bent (not straight in line with forearm)
coaching_cues: Let the string sit in the fingers
entries.score_impact: Creates inconsistent string release, causing horizontal and vertical grouping errors due to torque on the string.
entries.embed_text: draw anchor aim draw_wrist draw_hand Bent Draw Wrist relax hand straight wrist string sit fingers inconsistent release string torque grouping errors

entries.description: Variation in the draw hand's position against the face/jaw at full draw, which directly impacts the aiming reference and arrow trajectory. (estimated)
observable_signs: anchor_point_consistency measurement exceeding 5mm across multiple shots
coaching_cues: Feel the same spot every time
Don't move your head to the string

drill.name: Blind Anchor Drill
drill.description: Close your eyes or wear a blindfold and practice drawing to your anchor point, focusing purely on the tactile sensation of the string and hand against your face. Open eyes to check consistency.

entries.score_impact: Causes wide arrow groups and inconsistent vertical and horizontal arrow placement, making accuracy extremely difficult.
entries.embed_text: anchor draw_hand head anchor_point_consistency 5 20 0 3 mm Inconsistent anchor point Feel the same spot every time Solid anchor Don't move your head wide arrow groups inconsistent placement accuracy

entries.description: The archer tilts their head laterally or rotates it excessively, which can alter the sight picture, affect anchor consistency, and introduce torque. (estimated ranges)
observable_signs: head_tilt > 5 degrees from vertical or significant head rotation
entries.score_impact: Leads to inconsistent sight picture and anchor point, causing horizontal and vertical grouping errors.
entries.embed_text: setup draw anchor aim head spine head_tilt 6-30 degrees Head Tilt keep head still look through sight head to target inconsistent sight picture anchor grouping errors

entries.description: Engaging the large back muscles (rhomboids, latissimus dorsi) rather than biceps ensures a stronger, more consistent draw and reduces shoulder fatigue. This minimizes draw shoulder rise.
root_causes: Lack of awareness of back muscles
observable_signs: Draw shoulder rises significantly during the draw
Draw elbow collapses inward prematurely
Bow arm drops during draw
coaching_cues: Keep your shoulder down and back

drill.name: Resistance Band Draw
drill.description: Use a light resistance band to practice the drawing motion, focusing on initiating the pull with the back muscles and keeping the draw shoulder low. Hold at full draw for 3-5 seconds.

entries.score_impact: Leads to inconsistent draw length, poor anchor, and premature fatigue, causing vertical stringing and wide groups.
entries.embed_text: draw draw_shoulder spine draw_shoulder_rise 15-50mm error drawing with arm muscles pull with your back squeeze shoulder blades together inconsistent draw length poor anchor fatigue vertical stringing

entries.description: Starting the draw with the bow arm too low, requiring an upward movement during the draw, which can introduce inconsistency and tension. (observable sign)
root_causes: Lack of focus on consistent pre-draw routine
observable_signs: Bow arm visibly starting below target line, requiring a significant upward adjustment during draw
entries.score_impact: Can lead to inconsistent draw path and bow arm stability, contributing to minor grouping inconsistencies.
entries.embed_text: setup bow_shoulder bow_elbow Low Bow Arm Pre-Draw bow arm up first set frame ready to push inconsistent draw path minor grouping inconsistencies

entries.description: A fundamental principle for engaging the back muscles and maintaining a stable, powerful draw, preventing shoulder shrugging and promoting consistent anchor. (estimated ranges)
observable_signs: draw_shoulder_height_diff <= 0mm (draw shoulder at or below bow shoulder)
entries.score_impact: Crucial for consistent draw length, stable aiming, and a clean release, directly improving arrow grouping and accuracy.
entries.embed_text: draw anchor aim draw_shoulder spine draw_shoulder_height_diff -50-0mm Draw Shoulder Down & Back shoulder down back engage lats create space neck consistent draw stable aiming clean release improved grouping

entries.description: The draw elbow is positioned too high and often overly extended, causing the draw shoulder to rise and hindering proper back tension engagement. This creates an inefficient line of force. (estimated ranges)
root_causes: Trying to pull with arm muscles
observable_signs: Draw elbow angle (flexion) is greater than 100 degrees
Draw shoulder height is elevated relative to the bow shoulder
coaching_cues: Elbow in line with the arrow
entries.score_impact: Leads to inconsistent anchor, poor string clearance, and reduced power transfer, causing vertical and horizontal inconsistencies.
entries.embed_text: draw anchor aim draw_elbow draw_shoulder draw_elbow_angle 105-150 degrees high draw elbow chicken wing elbow in line with arrow drop elbow feel back muscles inconsistent anchor poor string clearance reduced power vertical horizontal inconsistencies

entries.description: The draw elbow is positioned too low and often overly flexed, which can lead to insufficient back tension, a collapsed draw arm, and an inefficient line of force. (estimated ranges)
root_causes: Trying to force the shoulder down too much
observable_signs: Draw elbow angle (flexion) is less than 90 degrees
Draw shoulder may be too low or forward
coaching_cues: Elbow in line with the arrow
entries.score_impact: Results in a weak and inconsistent anchor, difficulty achieving full draw length, and poor arrow flight due to lack of power and stability.
entries.embed_text: draw anchor aim draw_elbow draw_shoulder draw_elbow_angle 60-85 degrees low draw elbow lift elbow elbow in line with arrow engage back muscles weak inconsistent anchor poor arrow flight lack of power stability

entries.description: The draw elbow should be aligned with the arrow, forming a straight line of force from the elbow through the hand to the string, facilitating efficient back tension and stability. (estimated ranges)
observable_signs: Draw elbow angle (flexion) is consistently between 90-100 degrees
The elbow is spatially aligned with the arrow's path
coaching_cues: Elbow in line with the arrow
Maintain a strong line of force
Feel the connection to your back
entries.score_impact: Promotes consistent anchor, efficient power transfer, and improved accuracy by maximizing back tension and shot stability.
entries.embed_text: draw anchor aim draw_elbow draw_shoulder draw_elbow_angle 90-100 degrees optimal draw elbow alignment elbow in line with arrow maintain strong line consistent anchor efficient power improved accuracy

entries.description: The draw shoulder is raised towards the ear during the draw and anchor, indicating tension in the neck and upper trapezius, which prevents proper engagement of the back muscles. (estimated ranges)
root_causes: Trying to pull with arm/neck muscles
observable_signs: Draw shoulder height is significantly higher than the bow shoulder
Draw_shoulder_rise metric shows upward movement from setup to anchor
entries.score_impact: Causes significant inconsistency in anchor point and string clearance, leading to erratic arrow grouping and potential injury.
entries.embed_text: draw anchor aim draw_shoulder spine draw_shoulder_height_diff 20-50mm elevated draw shoulder shrugging shoulder down back relax neck use lats inconsistent anchor erratic grouping injury

entries.description: The draw shoulder rolls forward or drops excessively, losing the connection to the back muscles and compromising the skeletal alignment for stability and power. (estimated ranges)
root_causes: Trying to reach the anchor point
observable_signs: Draw shoulder height is significantly lower than the bow shoulder
Draw shoulder position is forward of the spine
entries.score_impact: Leads to a weak and inconsistent anchor, reduced power, and difficulty maintaining a stable aim, resulting in poor grouping and vertical inconsistencies.
entries.embed_text: draw anchor aim draw_shoulder spine draw_shoulder_height_diff -50 -10mm collapsed draw shoulder squeeze shoulder blades open chest shoulder back down weak inconsistent anchor reduced power poor grouping vertical inconsistencies

entries.description: Archery power and stability primarily come from engaging the large back muscles (rhomboids, latissimus dorsi) to pull the string, rather than relying on smaller arm muscles. This creates a stable platform for the shot.
observable_signs: Stable draw shoulder position
Minimal draw_shoulder_rise
Consistent draw_elbow_angle
Minimal spine movement during draw
entries.score_impact: Essential for consistent shot execution, improved accuracy, and injury prevention by distributing load efficiently and creating a repeatable release.
entries.embed_text: draw anchor aim draw_shoulder spine back tension principle pull with back squeeze shoulder blades feel lats engage consistent shot execution improved accuracy injury prevention

entries.description: Practice drawing the bow slowly with a resistance band or in front of a mirror, focusing on bringing the draw elbow directly in line with the arrow and maintaining the draw shoulder down and back throughout the movement.

drill.name: Resistance Band Draw
drill.description: Using a resistance band, slowly execute the draw cycle, pausing at full draw to check elbow and shoulder alignment in a mirror. Focus on feeling back muscle engagement.

entries.score_impact: Improves muscle memory for correct draw arm and shoulder position, leading to more consistent shot execution and better grouping over time.
entries.embed_text: draw anchor draw_elbow draw_shoulder draw_elbow_angle 90-100 degrees draw arm alignment drill resistance band draw slow controlled elbow to arrow shoulder down back muscle memory consistent shot execution better grouping

entries.description: Locking the bow arm elbow can lead to inconsistent arm position, increased tension, and potential injury from string slap or impact shock. (estimated)
root_causes: Lack of awareness of elbow rotation
observable_signs: Bow arm elbow angle close to or exceeding 180 degrees
Elbow joint visibly locked or concave
entries.score_impact: Causes inconsistent bow arm stability and can lead to string slap, resulting in erratic arrow flight and poor grouping.
entries.embed_text: setup bow_elbow bow_arm_extension_angle 178-185 degrees bow arm hyperextension soft elbow rotate elbow out don't lock elbow inconsistent bow arm stability string slap erratic arrow flight poor grouping

entries.description: Rotating the bow arm elbow outwards (away from the string) prevents string slap and promotes a more stable and consistent bow hold. (estimated)
observable_signs: Elbow joint facing towards the string
Forearm rotated inwards
coaching_cues: Point your elbow away from the string
entries.score_impact: Improves bow arm stability and prevents string contact, leading to cleaner arrow launch and better accuracy.
entries.embed_text: setup bow_elbow bow_arm_extension_angle bow arm elbow rotation rotate elbow out point elbow away from string soft elbow improves bow arm stability prevents string contact cleaner arrow launch better accuracy

entries.description: Shrugging the draw shoulder during the draw creates unnecessary tension in the neck and upper back, hindering proper back muscle engagement and consistent anchor. (estimated)
root_causes: Using arm muscles instead of back muscles
observable_signs: Draw shoulder height increasing significantly from setup to anchor
Draw shoulder visibly higher than bow shoulder
coaching_cues: Use your back, not your neck

drill.name: Shoulder Shrug Drill
drill.description: Practice drawing the bow while consciously keeping the shoulders depressed and relaxed. Focus on initiating the draw with the back muscles, not by shrugging.

entries.score_impact: Leads to increased tension, inconsistent anchor point, and poor release, severely impacting arrow grouping and accuracy.
entries.embed_text: draw draw_shoulder spine draw_shoulder_rise 10-50mm draw shoulder rise shoulders down relax shoulders use your back not your neck increased tension inconsistent anchor poor release poor grouping accuracy

entries.description: Initiating and executing the draw primarily with arm muscles (biceps) rather than engaging the back muscles (rhomboids, lats) leads to fatigue, tension, and poor alignment. (principle)
root_causes: Lack of understanding of back tension
observable_signs: Rapid flexion of the draw_elbow without significant scapular retraction
Draw shoulder rise
Lack of smooth, continuous movement
coaching_cues: Think about pulling your elbow back

drill.name: Resistance Band Draw
drill.description: Use a resistance band to practice the draw motion, focusing on engaging the back muscles and keeping the shoulders down. Perform slowly, feeling the scapular movement.

entries.score_impact: Causes fatigue, inconsistent draw length, and poor alignment, leading to erratic arrow flight and reduced accuracy.
entries.embed_text: draw draw_shoulder draw_elbow spine arm-dominant draw start with your back pull your elbow back squeeze your shoulder blades fatigue inconsistent draw length poor alignment erratic arrow flight reduced accuracy

entries.description: Engaging the back muscles (rhomboids, lats) throughout the draw and release provides power, stability, and a consistent expansion, which is fundamental for a good shot. (principle)
observable_signs: Stable draw_shoulder position (no rise)
Smooth, continuous draw motion
Scapular retraction visible

drill.name: Clicker Drill with Back Tension
drill.description: Draw the bow to anchor, then use only back muscle expansion to activate the clicker, maintaining a stable bow arm and head position.

entries.score_impact: Foundation of a powerful, consistent, and accurate shot, directly impacting arrow grouping and overall performance.
entries.embed_text: draw draw_shoulder spine back tension engagement squeeze your shoulder blades pull with your back expand through the shot powerful consistent accurate shot arrow grouping performance

entries.description: Dropping the draw elbow below the line of the arrow (chicken wing) reduces back muscle engagement, creates tension, and can lead to inconsistent string clearance. (estimated)
observable_signs: Draw elbow angle significantly larger than ideal at full draw
Draw elbow visibly lower than the arrow line
coaching_cues: Elbow in line with the arrow

drill.name: Mirror Draw Drill
drill.description: Practice drawing in front of a mirror, focusing on keeping the draw elbow high and in line with the arrow. Pay attention to the feeling of back muscle engagement.

entries.score_impact: Reduces power, causes inconsistent string clearance, and leads to poor accuracy due to inefficient force transfer.
entries.embed_text: draw draw_elbow draw_shoulder draw_elbow_angle 110-140 degrees draw elbow drop chicken wing elbow high elbow in line with arrow keep your elbow up reduces power inconsistent string clearance poor accuracy

entries.description: Variations in the draw hand's position against the face/jaw at full draw lead to inconsistent sight pictures and arrow trajectories. (estimated)
observable_signs: High standard deviation of draw_hand position relative to head/jaw across multiple shots
Visible shifts in draw_hand placement
coaching_cues: Touch the same spot every time
Feel the string on your face

drill.name: Blind Bale Anchor Drill
drill.description: Shoot at a blank bale from close range, focusing solely on achieving a consistent anchor point without aiming. Close your eyes briefly at full draw to enhance feel.

entries.score_impact: The primary cause of horizontal and vertical arrow dispersion, severely impacting grouping and overall accuracy.
entries.embed_text: anchor draw_hand head anchor_point_consistency 5-20mm inconsistent anchor point touch same spot every time feel string on face solid anchor primary cause horizontal vertical arrow dispersion poor grouping accuracy

entries.description: Leaning the spine significantly forward or backward disrupts the archer's balance and alignment, making it harder to maintain a stable aiming platform. (estimated)
observable_signs: Spine angle deviating more than 5 degrees from vertical
Visible forward or backward tilt of the torso

drill.name: Plumb Bob Stance Check
drill.description: Use a plumb bob or a vertical line on a wall to check your spine alignment during setup and draw. Adjust your stance to keep your spine vertical.

entries.score_impact: Leads to poor balance and an unstable aiming platform, causing inconsistent arrow placement, especially vertically.
entries.embed_text: aim spine hip spine_forward_lean -10-10 degrees spine lean forward back stand tall engage your core vertical spine poor balance unstable aiming platform inconsistent arrow placement vertically

entries.description: Tilting the head laterally (left or right) can lead to inconsistent anchor points and a distorted sight picture, affecting aiming accuracy. (estimated)
observable_signs: Head angle deviating more than 5 degrees from vertical
Visible tilt of the head relative to the spine

drill.name: Head Position Check
drill.description: Use a mirror or video feedback to ensure your head remains upright and still throughout the shot process, especially during anchor and aim.

entries.score_impact: Causes inconsistent anchor and sight alignment, leading to horizontal arrow dispersion and reduced accuracy.
entries.embed_text: aim head head_tilt -10-10 degrees head tilt head straight look through sight keep head still inconsistent anchor sight alignment horizontal arrow dispersion reduced accuracy

entries.description: Bending the bow arm elbow prematurely during or immediately after release causes the bow to move erratically, negatively affecting arrow flight. (estimated)
observable_signs: Bow_arm_extension_angle decreasing significantly (more flexion) from aim to follow-through
Visible bending of the bow elbow

drill.name: Hold and Push Drill
drill.description: After releasing the arrow, consciously hold your bow arm in position for an extra 2-3 seconds, focusing on pushing towards the target.

entries.score_impact: Leads to inconsistent arrow flight, poor grouping, and reduced accuracy due to bow movement during the critical launch phase.
entries.embed_text: release bow_elbow bow_shoulder bow_arm_extension_angle 140-165 degrees bow arm collapse hold bow arm strong push through shot maintain form inconsistent arrow flight poor grouping reduced accuracy

entries.description: Not maintaining body position after release for a sufficient duration indicates a lack of control and can lead to premature collapse of form, affecting consistency. (estimated)
observable_signs: Body position (spine, shoulders) changing rapidly after release
Follow-through duration less than 1 second
coaching_cues: Maintain your form until the arrow hits

drill.name: Counted Follow-Through
drill.description: After each shot, count to three slowly before relaxing your form. Focus on maintaining all body positions (bow arm, draw hand, spine, head).

entries.score_impact: Contributes to inconsistent shot execution and form breakdown, leading to erratic arrow placement and reduced accuracy.
entries.embed_text: follow_through spine bow_shoulder draw_shoulder follow_through_duration 0.1-0.5 seconds insufficient follow-through hold your finish stay in the shot maintain your form until arrow hits inconsistent shot execution form breakdown erratic arrow placement reduced accuracy

entries.description: The draw shoulder elevates significantly during the draw, indicating reliance on deltoid muscles instead of the larger back muscles for power. (estimated)
observable_signs: Draw shoulder height increases relative to bow shoulder
Draw elbow drops

drill.name: Resistance band draw
drill.description: Using a resistance band, practice the draw motion focusing on keeping the draw shoulder depressed and level with the bow shoulder throughout the movement.

entries.score_impact: Leads to inconsistent anchor point, poor back tension, and vertical stringing of arrows.
entries.embed_text: draw draw_shoulder bow_shoulder draw_shoulder_rise 5-50mm error shoulder rise shoulders down use your back stay level inconsistent anchor vertical stringing

entries.description: The draw elbow drops below the line of the arrow during the draw, indicating a reliance on bicep muscles rather than engaging the back and rotating the shoulder. (estimated)
observable_signs: Draw elbow visibly below arrow line
Draw shoulder may be lower than bow shoulder

drill.name: Wall draw drill
drill.description: Stand with your back against a wall, simulating the draw. Focus on keeping your draw elbow high and touching the wall as you draw back, engaging your back muscles.

entries.score_impact: Causes inconsistent anchor, poor string clearance, and difficulty achieving a clean release.
entries.embed_text: draw draw_elbow draw_shoulder draw_shoulder_height_diff -50 to -10mm error low draw elbow high elbow elbow back pull with your back inconsistent anchor poor string clearance

entries.description: The bow arm elbow bends significantly during the draw or at full draw, reducing stability and consistency of the bow hand position. (estimated)
observable_signs: Bow elbow angle decreases (becomes more bent) during draw or aim

drill.name: Hold at full draw
drill.description: Draw the bow to full anchor and hold the position for 5-10 seconds, focusing intently on maintaining a stable, extended bow arm without any collapse.

entries.score_impact: Leads to inconsistent sight picture, string slap, and horizontal grouping errors.
entries.embed_text: draw bow_elbow bow_arm_extension_angle 100-160 degrees error bow arm collapse push the bow straighten your arm maintain extension inconsistent sight picture string slap horizontal grouping

entries.description: The primary force for drawing the bow should come from the large muscles of the back (rhomboids, lats) by squeezing the shoulder blades together, not from the arm muscles.
observable_signs: Stable draw shoulder height
High draw elbow
Smooth draw path

drill.name: Resistance band draw
drill.description: Use a light resistance band to practice the draw, focusing on feeling the shoulder blades come together and the back muscles engaging, rather than just pulling with the arm.

entries.score_impact: Crucial for consistent draw length, stable anchor, and a clean, surprise release, leading to tighter groups.
entries.embed_text: draw anchor aim draw_shoulder spine principle back tension engage back tension squeeze shoulder blades pull with your back consistent draw length stable anchor clean release

entries.description: The archer's head tilts laterally or forward/backward during the draw or at anchor, often in an attempt to reach the string or compensate for poor body alignment. (estimated)
observable_signs: Head angle deviates from vertical
Chin may lift or drop
coaching_cues: Let the string come to you

drill.name: Mirror drill
drill.description: Practice the draw and anchor in front of a mirror, paying close attention to keeping the head perfectly still and upright throughout the entire shot sequence.

entries.score_impact: Leads to inconsistent anchor point, varying sight picture, and inconsistent vertical arrow placement.
entries.embed_text: draw anchor aim head head_tilt 5-20 degrees error head tilt keep your head still look straight ahead inconsistent anchor varying sight picture vertical arrow placement

entries.description: The archer's spine tilts sideways during the draw or at full draw, often to compensate for strength imbalances or to get the string to the face. (estimated)
observable_signs: Spine angle deviates laterally from vertical
Shoulders may not be level

drill.name: Plumb line check
drill.description: Set up a plumb line next to your shooting position. Draw the bow and observe your posture, ensuring your spine remains vertically aligned with the plumb line.

entries.score_impact: Severely impacts balance, consistency of anchor, and leads to wide horizontal grouping of arrows.
entries.embed_text: draw anchor aim spine hip spine_lateral_tilt 5-15 degrees error spine lateral tilt stand tall stay upright engage core balance anchor horizontal grouping

entries.description: The draw hand should move in a consistent, straight line from the pre-draw position directly back to the anchor point, avoiding loops or erratic movements.
observable_signs: Draw hand trajectory is linear and repeatable across shots

drill.name: Blind bale draw
drill.description: Draw the bow with eyes closed towards a blank bale, focusing solely on the feel of a smooth, straight draw path to the anchor point.

entries.score_impact: Ensures consistent draw length and anchor, which is fundamental for accuracy and tight groupings.
entries.embed_text: draw draw_hand draw_elbow draw_shoulder technique_cue consistent draw path smooth straight draw path straight back smooth draw consistent draw length anchor accuracy

entries.description: Maintaining a consistent bow arm extension angle throughout the draw and aim phases is crucial for a stable sight picture and repeatable shot execution. (estimated)
observable_signs: Bow elbow angle remains stable from full draw to release

drill.name: Mirror hold
drill.description: Draw to full anchor in front of a mirror and hold, visually checking that the bow arm remains consistently extended and stable without any movement or collapse.

entries.score_impact: Directly affects sight picture stability and horizontal consistency of arrow groups.
entries.embed_text: draw anchor aim bow_elbow bow_arm_extension_angle 170-180 degrees technique_cue consistent bow arm extension lock your bow arm push through the shot sight picture stability horizontal consistency

entries.description: Actively depressing the draw shoulder during the draw helps engage the back muscles and prevents the shoulder from rising, promoting better alignment and power transfer. (estimated)
observable_signs: Draw shoulder height remains stable or slightly decreases relative to bow shoulder

drill.name: Shoulder shrugs
drill.description: Practice shrugging your shoulders up and then actively depressing them down, focusing on the feeling of engaging the muscles that pull the shoulder blade down and back.

entries.score_impact: Contributes to a stable anchor, efficient power transfer, and reduces vertical stringing.
entries.embed_text: draw anchor draw_shoulder draw_shoulder_rise -5-0mm technique_cue depress draw shoulder shoulder down relax neck stable anchor efficient power transfer vertical stringing

entries.description: Achieving the exact same anchor point on the face/jaw for every shot is paramount for repeatable aiming and accuracy. (estimated)
observable_signs: Draw hand position relative to head/jaw varies minimally across shots

drill.name: Blind bale anchor
drill.description: Draw and anchor with eyes closed towards a blank bale, focusing intensely on the tactile feedback of the string and hand against the face to ensure a consistent anchor point.

entries.score_impact: Directly impacts vertical and horizontal consistency of arrow placement on the target.
entries.embed_text: anchor aim draw_hand head anchor_point_consistency 0-2mm technique_cue consistent anchor point feel same spot solid contact repeatable aiming accuracy vertical horizontal consistency

entries.description: Leaning too far forward from the waist can disrupt balance, strain the lower back, and make it difficult to achieve proper back tension and a stable anchor. (estimated)
observable_signs: Spine angle deviates significantly forward from vertical

drill.name: Wall stance drill
drill.description: Stand with your back against a wall, then step slightly forward, maintaining a straight back and only a minimal forward lean from the ankles, not the waist.

entries.score_impact: Reduces stability, makes consistent aiming difficult, and can lead to inconsistent vertical arrow placement.
entries.embed_text: setup draw anchor spine hip spine_forward_lean 10-25 degrees error excessive forward lean stand tall hips under shoulders stability aiming vertical arrow placement

entries.description: The draw wrist should be flat and in line with the forearm, avoiding excessive bending or hyperextension, to ensure consistent string release and prevent plucking.
observable_signs: Draw wrist angle appears straight, not bent up or down

drill.name: Finger sling draw
drill.description: Practice drawing the bow using only a finger sling, which encourages a relaxed hand and a naturally flat wrist position, preventing gripping or bending.

entries.score_impact: Crucial for a clean release, preventing string plucking, and ensuring consistent horizontal arrow flight.
entries.embed_text: draw anchor draw_wrist draw_hand technique_cue flat draw wrist straight line to elbow clean release string plucking consistent horizontal arrow flight

entries.description: The bow shoulder should be kept down and relaxed, not shrugged up, to create a stable platform for the bow arm and allow for proper back engagement.
observable_signs: Bow shoulder height remains low and stable relative to the neck/head

drill.name: Bow arm extension drill
drill.description: Practice extending the bow arm forward as if pushing the bow towards the target, focusing on keeping the bow shoulder down and relaxed, avoiding any shrugging.

entries.score_impact: Enhances bow stability, reduces tension, and contributes to a more consistent sight picture and shot execution.
entries.embed_text: setup draw bow_shoulder spine technique_cue depressed bow shoulder bow shoulder down relax bow arm bow stability sight picture shot execution

entries.description: The entire draw sequence should be executed smoothly and with control, avoiding jerky movements or rushing, to allow for proper muscle engagement and consistent timing.
observable_signs: Joint velocities are smooth and consistent throughout the draw phase

drill.name: Clicker drill
drill.description: Practice drawing the bow to the clicker, focusing on a smooth, continuous movement that allows the clicker to fall naturally without rushing or hesitation.

entries.score_impact: Essential for consistent shot timing, muscle memory development, and overall accuracy.
entries.embed_text: draw draw_hand draw_elbow draw_shoulder bow_hand bow_elbow bow_shoulder principle smooth controlled draw slow steady controlled movement consistent shot timing muscle memory accuracy

entries.description: The hips should be consistently aligned relative to the target line, typically in a square or slightly open stance, to provide a stable base and facilitate proper upper body rotation. (estimated)
observable_signs: Hip angle relative to target line is stable and within ideal range

drill.name: Foot placement markers
drill.description: Use tape or markers on the ground to establish a consistent foot position for each shot, ensuring repeatable hip and body alignment relative to the target.

entries.score_impact: Forms the foundation for a stable shot, influencing balance and upper body alignment, which impacts horizontal arrow grouping.
entries.embed_text: setup hip knee hip_rotation -5-5 degrees technique_cue consistent hip rotation square hips open stance stable shot balance horizontal grouping

entries.description: The bow arm is positioned too high or too low during the setup phase, leading to an inconsistent aiming platform and potential vertical stringing. (estimated)
root_causes: Trying to compensate for other errors
observable_signs: Bow shoulder significantly higher or lower than the ideal horizontal line relative to the target
coaching_cues: Bow arm parallel to the ground

drill.name: Mirror Setup Check
drill.description: Stand in front of a mirror and practice your setup, ensuring your bow arm is consistently level with the ground. Repeat until muscle memory is established.

entries.score_impact: Causes vertical inconsistencies in arrow placement due to an unstable or varying aiming platform.
entries.embed_text: setup bow_shoulder bow_elbow bow_shoulder_height_diff -50 50 -10 10 mm Incorrect bow arm height Bow arm parallel to the ground Level your bow arm vertical inconsistencies

entries.description: Relying primarily on arm muscles (biceps/triceps) instead of engaging the back muscles to pull the string, leading to inefficient power transfer, fatigue, and inconsistent draw length. (estimated)
observable_signs: Significant draw_shoulder_rise during the draw phase
Draw elbow collapsing early or dropping
Increased spine_forward_lean
coaching_cues: Think about pulling with your shoulder blade
Keep your elbow high and back

drill.name: Resistance Band Draw
drill.description: Use a light resistance band to practice the draw motion, focusing on initiating the pull with the back muscles and keeping the draw shoulder down. Hold at full draw for 3-5 seconds.

entries.score_impact: Leads to inconsistent draw length, poor accuracy, and premature fatigue, severely impacting group size and consistency.
entries.embed_text: draw draw_shoulder draw_elbow spine draw_shoulder_rise 10 50 0 5 mm Arm drawing Muscling the bow Initiate with your back Pull with your shoulder blade Keep your elbow high inconsistent draw length poor accuracy fatigue

entries.description: The draw shoulder elevates significantly during the draw, often indicating over-reliance on arm muscles or tension in the neck and trapezius, hindering proper back engagement. (estimated)
observable_signs: draw_shoulder_rise measurement exceeding 10mm from setup to anchor
coaching_cues: Pull with your back, not your shoulder

drill.name: Shoulder Awareness Drill
drill.description: Perform slow draws without an arrow, focusing intently on keeping the draw shoulder low and relaxed throughout the entire draw cycle. Use a mirror or partner for feedback.

entries.score_impact: Contributes to an inconsistent anchor, vertical stringing, and increased fatigue, making consistent aiming difficult.
entries.embed_text: draw draw_shoulder draw_shoulder_rise 10 50 0 5 mm Draw shoulder rising Keep your draw shoulder down Relax your neck Pull with your back inconsistent anchor vertical stringing fatigue

entries.description: The draw elbow drops below the line of the arrow or shoulder during the draw, reducing effective back muscle engagement and creating an inefficient force vector for the pull. (estimated)
observable_signs: draw_elbow_angle (upper arm relative to horizontal) significantly below 0 degrees
coaching_cues: Keep your elbow in line with the arrow

drill.name: Wall Draw Drill
drill.description: Stand with your back against a wall, simulating the draw. Focus on keeping your draw elbow high and touching the wall as you draw, ensuring it doesn't drop. Do not use a bow.

entries.score_impact: Leads to reduced power, inconsistent anchor, and horizontal stringing, making consistent arrow grouping challenging.
entries.embed_text: draw draw_elbow draw_shoulder draw_elbow_angle -10 -30 0 15 degrees Draw elbow dropping Elbow high and back Lead with your elbow Keep your elbow in line with the arrow reduced power inconsistent anchor horizontal stringing

entries.description: Actively pushing the bow arm towards the target during the draw helps engage the bow shoulder, maintain a stable front side, and create opposing forces for a clean release. (estimated)
observable_signs: Consistent bow_arm_extension_angle throughout the draw and aim phases
Stable bow_shoulder position
coaching_cues: Reach out with your bow hand

drill.name: Bow Arm Extension Hold
drill.description: At full draw, focus on actively pushing the bow arm towards the target for 3-5 seconds before releasing. This builds strength and awareness for the 'push' action.

entries.score_impact: Improves overall stability, consistency, and follow-through, contributing to tighter arrow groups.
entries.embed_text: draw bow_shoulder bow_elbow bow_arm_extension_angle 160 180 170 178 degrees Active bow arm push Push into the target Extend your bow arm Reach out improves stability consistency

entries.description: Tilting, turning, or moving the head during the draw phase disrupts the visual aiming reference and leads to an inconsistent anchor point. (estimated)
observable_signs: head_tilt changing by more than 5 degrees from setup to anchor
Head position shifting horizontally or vertically
coaching_cues: Anchor to your face, don't move your face to the string

drill.name: Head Stillness Drill
drill.description: Place a small piece of tape on a wall at eye level. Practice drawing while keeping your gaze fixed on the tape, ensuring no head movement. Use a mirror for self-correction.

entries.score_impact: Results in an inconsistent anchor and aiming, causing both vertical and horizontal stringing of arrows.
entries.embed_text: draw head spine head_tilt 5 20 0 2 degrees Head tilting moving during draw Keep your head still Look straight ahead Anchor to your face inconsistent anchor vertical horizontal stringing

entries.description: Establishing a consistent pre-draw position, with the draw hand at eye level and the bow arm parallel to the ground, sets up an efficient and repeatable draw sequence. (estimated)
observable_signs: Draw hand at consistent height relative to the head
Bow arm angle consistent and level

drill.name: Pre-Draw Repetition
drill.description: Practice only the setup and pre-draw phases repeatedly, focusing on hitting the exact same starting position for your draw hand and bow arm each time. Use a mirror for visual feedback.

entries.score_impact: Improves the consistency of the entire shot sequence, leading to more predictable and accurate shots.
entries.embed_text: setup draw_hand bow_hand bow_shoulder draw_shoulder_height_diff -20 20 -5 5 mm Consistent pre-draw setup Draw hand to eye level Bow arm level Consistent start position improves consistency accuracy

entries.description: Consistently reaching the full, biomechanically correct draw length is essential for consistent arrow speed, trajectory, and proper back tension engagement. (estimated)
observable_signs: Draw hand consistently reaching the established anchor point
Stable body position at anchor without collapsing

drill.name: Draw Length Check
drill.description: Use a clicker or a visual marker on your arrow to ensure you are consistently reaching the same draw length every shot. Focus on feeling the 'wall' of your draw.

entries.score_impact: Ensures consistent arrow speed and trajectory, which is fundamental for accurate vertical arrow placement.
entries.embed_text: anchor draw_shoulder draw_elbow draw_hand anchor_point_height 0 0 0 0 mm Achieving full draw length Pull all the way back Feel the wall Reach your anchor consistent arrow speed trajectory accurate vertical placement

entries.description: Maintaining a high draw elbow, roughly in line with the arrow, facilitates proper back tension and ensures a consistent anchor point. A low elbow indicates poor back engagement (estimated ranges).
observable_signs: Draw elbow angle is too acute (collapsed)
Draw shoulder is higher than the bow shoulder
coaching_cues: Point your elbow at the target
Line up your elbow with the arrow

drill.name: Mirror Draw Check
drill.description: Stand in front of a mirror and practice your draw, paying close attention to the height and angle of your draw elbow. Ensure it stays high and aligned with the arrow path.

entries.score_impact: Hinders proper back tension, leads to an inconsistent anchor, and can cause string clearance issues, affecting arrow grouping.
entries.embed_text: draw draw_elbow draw_shoulder draw_elbow_angle 60-85 degrees error low draw elbow elbow up and back line up elbow with arrow inconsistent anchor string clearance

entries.description: Locking or hyperextending the bow elbow can lead to string slap, injury, and inconsistent bow arm stability. A slight bend (170-178 degrees) is ideal for shock absorption and safety (estimated ranges).
root_causes: Lack of awareness of elbow position
observable_signs: Bow arm elbow angle is greater than 180 degrees
Elbow joint appears 'locked'
coaching_cues: Slight bend in your bow arm

drill.name: Elbow Bend Check
drill.description: Practice drawing and holding, consciously maintaining a slight bend in your bow arm. Have a partner or camera check your elbow angle from the side to ensure it's not locked.

entries.score_impact: Increases risk of string slap on the forearm, causes inconsistent bow arm stability, and can lead to joint injury, impacting accuracy.
entries.embed_text: draw bow_elbow bow_arm_extension_angle 181-190 degrees error hyperextended bow elbow soft elbow slight bend in bow arm string slap injury inconsistent bow arm stability

entries.description: Leaning back during the draw compromises balance and makes it difficult to engage the back muscles effectively. A neutral, vertical spine posture is crucial for stability and consistent force application (estimated ranges).
root_causes: Attempting to compensate for heavy draw weight
observable_signs: Spine forward lean angle is significantly negative (leaning away from target)
Hip position shifts backward

drill.name: Wall Stance Drill
drill.description: Stand with your back against a wall, ensuring your heels, hips, and shoulders touch the wall. Practice your draw motion while maintaining contact with the wall to reinforce a vertical spine.

entries.score_impact: Leads to balance issues, inconsistent anchor point, and difficulty achieving proper back tension, resulting in erratic arrow placement.
entries.embed_text: draw spine hip spine_forward_lean -15 to -5 degrees error spine leaning back stand tall maintain vertical posture balance issues inconsistent anchor erratic arrow placement

entries.description: Tilting the head sideways at anchor creates an inconsistent reference point for the string and sight picture, making consistent aiming difficult. The head should remain upright and stable (estimated ranges).
root_causes: Trying to 'find' the anchor point with the head
observable_signs: Head tilt angle is significantly non-zero (tilted left or right)

drill.name: Head Position Check
drill.description: Practice drawing and anchoring in front of a mirror, ensuring your head remains perfectly upright and level. Focus on feeling the string contact points without moving your head.

entries.score_impact: Directly causes an inconsistent anchor point and sight picture, leading to horizontal stringing and wide groups on the target.
entries.embed_text: anchor head spine head_tilt 5-15 degrees error head tilting sideways head up and still look straight ahead inconsistent anchor sight picture horizontal stringing

entries.description: A bent or 'broken' draw wrist at anchor compromises the consistency of the string release and can alter the draw length. A flat wrist ensures a clean release and consistent anchor pressure.
root_causes: Incorrect hand position on the string
observable_signs: Draw wrist angle is significantly flexed or extended
Knuckles are not in line with the forearm
coaching_cues: Straight line from knuckles to elbow

drill.name: Flat Wrist Drill
drill.description: Practice drawing and anchoring, focusing on keeping the draw wrist perfectly flat. Use a small ruler or straight edge placed on the back of your hand and forearm to check for straightness.

entries.score_impact: Leads to inconsistent string release, affecting arrow flight and causing vertical or horizontal stringing on the target.
entries.embed_text: anchor draw_wrist draw_hand error broken draw wrist flat wrist straight line from knuckles to elbow inconsistent string release vertical horizontal stringing

entries.description: A smooth, continuous draw motion prevents muscle fatigue and ensures consistent shot timing. Stopping or stuttering mid-draw disrupts rhythm and increases effort.
root_causes: Insufficient draw weight for archer's strength
observable_signs: Velocity of draw_hand or draw_elbow drops to zero or near zero mid-draw
Jerky movements in joint angles during draw

drill.name: Slow Motion Draw
drill.description: Practice drawing the bow very slowly and deliberately, focusing on maintaining continuous movement without any pauses or jerks. Feel the engagement of your back muscles throughout.

entries.score_impact: Causes inconsistent shot timing, increased muscle fatigue, and difficulty achieving a consistent anchor, leading to varied arrow placement.
entries.embed_text: draw draw_shoulder draw_elbow bow_shoulder bow_elbow error stuttering draw motion smooth and continuous one fluid motion inconsistent shot timing fatigue varied arrow placement

entries.description: Raising the bow arm to the correct height before initiating the draw ensures proper alignment and reduces unnecessary movement during the draw phase. Starting low requires extra effort and movement.
root_causes: Lack of awareness of pre-draw position
Attempting to 'push' the bow up during the draw
observable_signs: Bow arm starts significantly below target height
Bow shoulder rises considerably during the initial draw phase
coaching_cues: Get to full height before pulling

drill.name: Pre-Draw Hold
drill.description: Nock an arrow and raise your bow arm to the correct height and extension, holding it steady for 2-3 seconds before starting your draw. Focus on stability.

entries.score_impact: Leads to inconsistent draw mechanics, potential for poor alignment, and increased effort, which can affect shot consistency over time.
entries.embed_text: setup bow_shoulder bow_elbow draw_shoulder draw_shoulder_height_diff 30-60mm error bow arm starts low set your bow arm first bow arm up then draw inconsistent draw mechanics poor alignment

entries.description: Minimizing the upward movement of the draw shoulder during the draw is critical for engaging the back muscles and maintaining a stable anchor. Excessive rise indicates arm-dominant drawing (estimated ranges).
observable_signs: Draw shoulder height increases significantly from setup to anchor
Draw shoulder appears 'shrugged'

drill.name: Shoulder Depression Drill
drill.description: Practice drawing while consciously trying to push your draw shoulder down and back. Use a mirror to observe and ensure minimal upward movement of the shoulder.

entries.score_impact: Compromises back tension, leads to an inconsistent anchor height, increases fatigue, and can cause injury, severely impacting accuracy and consistency.
entries.embed_text: draw draw_shoulder draw_shoulder_rise 20-50mm error excessive draw shoulder rise keep your shoulder down depress your shoulder blade poor back tension inconsistent anchor fatigue injury

entries.description: A consistent anchor point, where the string hand repeatedly touches the same spot on the face/jaw, is fundamental for repeatable aiming and arrow placement. Variability leads to wide groups (estimated ranges).
observable_signs: High standard deviation in draw_hand 3D position at full draw across multiple shots
Variations in head_tilt or draw_wrist angle at anchor
coaching_cues: Don't move your head to find the anchor

drill.name: Blind Bale Anchor
drill.description: Shoot at a blank bale from a close distance (3-5m) with eyes closed or blurred vision, focusing solely on feeling the anchor point consistently. Open eyes only after release.

entries.score_impact: The most critical factor for accuracy; inconsistency in the anchor point directly causes wide groups, especially vertical and horizontal stringing.

entries.description: The draw elbow drops below the line of the arrow, hindering proper back tension and creating an unstable anchor. (Estimated ranges)

Trying to 'pull' rather than 'push and pull'
observable_signs: Draw elbow significantly lower than the arrow line
Draw shoulder appears rotated forward
coaching_cues: Point your elbow at the wall behind you
entries.score_impact: Prevents consistent back tension, leading to poor release, inconsistent anchor, and erratic arrow grouping.

entries.description: Back tension involves engaging the muscles between the shoulder blades to expand the back, rather than just pulling with the arm muscles. This creates a stable and powerful draw.
observable_signs: Consistent rearward movement of the draw shoulder and elbow during the draw and aim phases, indicating engagement of back muscles.
coaching_cues: Pull with your back, not your arm.
entries.score_impact: Improves shot consistency and power, leading to tighter groups and better arrow flight.

entries.description: The draw elbow is positioned too low or too far forward at full draw, indicating a lack of proper back tension and engagement of the shoulder blade muscles (estimated ranges). This weakens the shot.

Draw shoulder appears higher than bow shoulder
coaching_cues: Get your elbow behind the arrow.

drill.name: Resistance Band Pulls
drill.description: Using a resistance band, practice pulling to anchor with a high, back elbow, focusing on squeezing the shoulder blades. Hold for 3-5 seconds.

entries.score_impact: Reduces power and consistency, leading to vertical stringing and inconsistent anchor points.

entries.description: After release, the string hand should move straight back along the jawline, as if "painting the face," indicating a passive and consistent release driven by back tension.
observable_signs: Draw hand path moves directly rearward along the face/jawline post-release
Draw elbow moves rearward and slightly outward

drill.name: Mirror Follow-Through
drill.description: Practice releases in front of a mirror, focusing on the string hand moving straight back along the jawline without dropping or moving sideways.

entries.score_impact: Ensures a clean string clearance and consistent shot execution, improving horizontal grouping.
entries.embed_text: follow_through draw_hand draw_elbow draw_shoulder straight back follow-through paint face hand straight back passive consistent release clean string clearance consistent shot execution horizontal grouping

entries.description: The string hand moves out to the side, down, or away from the face after release, indicating an active release, plucking the string, or a collapse of back tension (estimated ranges).

Draw elbow drops or moves sideways

drill.name: Wall Drill Follow-Through
drill.description: Stand with your back to a wall, simulating a shot. The string hand should brush the wall as it moves straight back, preventing lateral or downward movement.

entries.score_impact: Causes inconsistent string clearance and shot execution, leading to erratic arrow flight and poor grouping.

Continuous rearward movement of draw hand/shoulder through release

drill.name: Finger Sling Drill
drill.description: Use a finger sling to hold the bow, allowing the hand to relax completely at release. Focus on the string pulling the fingers open, not actively opening them.

entries.score_impact: Promotes a clean string departure and reduces torque on the string, leading to more consistent arrow flight and tighter groups.

Bow shoulder drops or moves forward

drill.name: Bow Arm Hold Drill
drill.description: After each shot, hold the bow arm perfectly still and extended for 3-5 seconds, focusing on stability and preventing any collapse.

entries.score_impact: Causes inconsistent arrow impact points, especially vertically, due to varying bow torque and energy absorption.

Head position shifts relative to spine

drill.name: Target Stare Drill
drill.description: After releasing the arrow, keep your eyes fixed on the target for 3-5 seconds, resisting the urge to move your head or follow the arrow.

entries.score_impact: Causes inconsistent aiming and follow-through, leading to erratic arrow placement, particularly horizontally.

entries.description: Actively pushing the bow arm forward or into the shot at release, rather than maintaining a stable, strong position. This creates unnecessary movement and torque (estimated ranges).

Bow arm extension angle momentarily increases beyond ideal

drill.name: Bow Arm Stability Drill
drill.description: Focus on maintaining a completely static bow arm from anchor through release and follow-through. Imagine your bow arm is a statue.

entries.score_impact: Introduces significant torque and inconsistent forces into the shot, leading to wide groups and unpredictable arrow flight.

entries.description: Shooting at a blank bale from a very close distance (e.g., 3-5 meters) without aiming or with eyes closed. This drill helps archers focus purely on the feel of their shot execution and back tension, removing visual distractions.
observable_signs: Consistent draw shoulder movement
Stable draw elbow position
Smooth follow-through

drill.name: Blind Bailing
drill.description: Shoot arrows into a blank bale from 3-5 meters, either with eyes closed or looking away. Concentrate solely on the physical sensation of a good draw, anchor, and release.

entries.score_impact: Improves proprioception and muscle memory for consistent shot execution, leading to better form and reduced target panic.
entries.embed_text: aim draw_shoulder draw_elbow draw_hand blind bailing drill blank bale close distance no aiming feel shot execution back tension proprioception muscle memory consistent shot execution reduced target panic

entries.description: A drill where the archer focuses on continuing to pull through the clicker, using back tension, rather than stopping or anticipating the click. This reinforces a continuous, active draw.
observable_signs: Continuous rearward movement of the draw shoulder and elbow until the clicker sounds
Minimal pause at full draw before release

drill.name: Clicker Drill
drill.description: At full draw, focus on maintaining continuous back tension and pulling through the clicker until it drops. Avoid stopping or "waiting" for the click.

entries.score_impact: Develops a consistent and active release sequence, preventing target panic and improving shot timing and consistency.
entries.embed_text: aim draw_shoulder draw_elbow clicker drill pull through clicker continuous active draw consistent rearward movement draw shoulder minimal pause full draw develop consistent active release sequence prevent target panic improve shot timing consistency

entries.description: Actively engaging and squeezing the shoulder blades together during the draw and anchor phases helps to properly load the back muscles and stabilize the upper body. This prevents shoulder rise and promotes a strong, consistent draw (estimated range).
observable_signs: Draw shoulder moves rearward and slightly downward relative to the bow shoulder
Reduced draw_shoulder_rise from setup to anchor
coaching_cues: Pull your shoulder down and back.

drill.name: Scapular Retraction Holds
drill.description: Without the bow, practice squeezing your shoulder blades together and holding for 5-10 seconds. Focus on feeling the muscles engage.

entries.score_impact: Improves draw stability, reduces shoulder fatigue, and contributes to a more consistent anchor and release, enhancing overall accuracy.
entries.embed_text: draw draw_shoulder spine draw_shoulder_height_diff engage shoulder blades squeeze shoulder blades open chest pull shoulder down back scapular retraction holds improves draw stability reduces shoulder fatigue consistent anchor release overall accuracy

entries.description: Bending the draw wrist breaks the straight line from the elbow to the arrow, reducing consistency and efficient power transfer. This can lead to string slap and an inconsistent anchor point.
observable_signs: Draw wrist angle significantly deviates from a straight line with the forearm and hand.
coaching_cues: Straight line from elbow to arrow

drill.name: Mirror check
drill.description: Practice drawing in front of a mirror, focusing on keeping the draw wrist flat and in line with the forearm and arrow. Ensure the hand is relaxed.

entries.score_impact: Causes inconsistent arrow flight due to varied string contact and an inconsistent anchor point, leading to vertical and horizontal dispersion.
entries.embed_text: recurve draw anchor aim draw_wrist draw_hand bent draw wrist inconsistent string contact anchor point vertical horizontal dispersion keep wrist flat straight line elbow arrow relax fingers

entries.description: A collapsed draw elbow means the elbow angle is too acute, indicating a lack of proper back tension and an over-reliance on arm muscles. This weakens the draw and makes the anchor unstable. (Ranges estimated)
observable_signs: Draw elbow angle is significantly smaller than ideal
Elbow drops below the arrow line.

drill.name: Resistance band draw
drill.description: Use a resistance band to practice the draw, focusing on pulling with the back muscles and maintaining a consistent, strong draw elbow angle throughout the draw and anchor.

entries.score_impact: Leads to inconsistent draw length and anchor, causing significant vertical and horizontal arrow dispersion due to varied power and release.
entries.embed_text: recurve draw anchor aim draw_elbow draw_shoulder draw_elbow_angle collapsed draw elbow insufficient back tension unstable anchor inconsistent draw length vertical horizontal dispersion engage back muscles pull elbow back maintain strong elbow angle

entries.description: A draw elbow that is too low at anchor indicates the draw shoulder is dropped, which can lead to poor back tension engagement and an inconsistent string path. This reduces power and stability. (Ranges estimated)
observable_signs: Draw elbow visibly below the arrow line
Draw shoulder significantly lower than bow shoulder.

drill.name: Wall draw
drill.description: Stand with your back against a wall, ensuring your draw elbow stays level with the arrow line as you draw. Focus on engaging your back muscles to achieve the correct height.

entries.score_impact: Results in inconsistent string clearance and release, causing vertical arrow dispersion and potential string slap.
entries.embed_text: recurve anchor aim draw_elbow draw_shoulder draw_shoulder_height_diff low draw elbow dropped draw shoulder poor back tension inconsistent string path vertical dispersion elbow level arrow lift elbow slightly engage back pull

entries.description: A draw elbow that is too high at anchor often indicates a shrugged draw shoulder, leading to tension in the neck and upper traps, hindering proper back tension and a smooth release. (Ranges estimated)
root_causes: Trying to achieve a high anchor point
observable_signs: Draw elbow visibly above the arrow line
Draw shoulder significantly higher than bow shoulder.
coaching_cues: Pull your shoulder blade back and down

drill.name: Shoulder awareness drill
drill.description: Before drawing, consciously drop and relax your draw shoulder. As you draw, focus on keeping it low and engaged with your back muscles, avoiding any shrugging.

entries.score_impact: Creates tension that can lead to an inconsistent release and follow-through, causing vertical and horizontal arrow dispersion.
entries.embed_text: recurve anchor aim draw_elbow draw_shoulder draw_shoulder_height_diff high draw elbow shrugged draw shoulder neck tension inconsistent release vertical horizontal dispersion keep shoulder down relax neck pull shoulder blade back down

entries.description: Excessive draw shoulder rise during the draw indicates a reliance on arm and upper trap muscles instead of engaging the back muscles. This creates tension and instability at full draw. (Ranges estimated)
observable_signs: Draw shoulder visibly elevates during the draw phase compared to the setup position.
coaching_cues: Keep your shoulder down as you draw
Initiate the draw from your shoulder blade

drill.name: Band draw with mirror
drill.description: Using a resistance band, perform slow draws in front of a mirror, focusing on keeping the draw shoulder low and stable throughout the entire draw motion. Feel the engagement in your back.

entries.score_impact: Leads to an unstable anchor and inconsistent release, causing significant vertical and horizontal arrow dispersion due to varied force application and body tension.
entries.embed_text: recurve draw draw_shoulder spine draw_shoulder_rise excessive draw shoulder rise arm muscles upper trap tension instability unstable anchor inconsistent release vertical horizontal dispersion pull back keep shoulder down initiate draw shoulder blade

entries.description: An inconsistent string hook (how fingers engage the string) directly impacts the anchor point and release, leading to variations in draw length and string path. (Ranges estimated)
observable_signs: Variations in draw hand position at anchor
Slight changes in draw wrist angle.
coaching_cues: Let the string sit in the hook

drill.name: Blind bale drill
drill.description: Shoot at a blank bale from a close distance, focusing solely on achieving a consistent string hook and anchor point without aiming. Pay attention to the feel of the string on your fingers.

entries.score_impact: Directly causes inconsistent draw length and anchor, leading to significant vertical and horizontal arrow dispersion.
entries.embed_text: recurve anchor aim draw_hand draw_wrist anchor_point_consistency inconsistent string hook varied draw length string path vertical horizontal dispersion deep consistent hook relax fingers string sit hook

entries.description: Engaging the back muscles (rhomboids, lower traps) for the draw, rather than arm muscles, ensures a stable, repeatable draw and a powerful, clean release. This minimizes shoulder tension. (Ranges estimated for non-engagement)
observable_signs: Minimal draw shoulder rise
Stable draw elbow position
Draw shoulder pulled back and down.

drill.name: Shoulder blade squeeze
drill.description: Without a bow, practice squeezing your shoulder blades together and down, feeling the muscles in your upper back engage. Integrate this feeling into your draw motion.

entries.score_impact: Improves consistency, power, and accuracy by creating a stable platform for the shot and a smooth, surprise release.
entries.embed_text: recurve draw anchor aim draw_shoulder spine draw_shoulder_rise back tension engagement stable repeatable draw powerful clean release minimal shoulder tension consistency power accuracy pull back squeeze shoulder blades feel lats engage

entries.description: Maintaining a straight line from the draw elbow through the wrist to the arrow ensures efficient power transfer and a consistent string path, crucial for accuracy. (Ideal range for elbow bend estimated)
observable_signs: Draw elbow, wrist, and hand appear aligned with the arrow shaft.
coaching_cues: Straight line from elbow to arrow

drill.name: Visual alignment check
drill.description: Use a mirror or video feedback to visually confirm the straight line from your draw elbow to the arrow, ensuring no breaks at the wrist or hand.

entries.score_impact: Enhances shot consistency and reduces string interference, leading to tighter arrow groups.
entries.embed_text: recurve draw anchor aim draw_elbow draw_wrist draw_hand draw_elbow_angle straight draw arm line efficient power transfer consistent string path accuracy tighter groups straight line elbow arrow keep wrist flat don't break wrist

entries.description: A consistent anchor point, where the draw hand repeatedly touches the same spot on the face, is fundamental for repeatable aiming and accurate arrow placement. (Ranges estimated)
observable_signs: Minimal variation in draw hand position relative to the head at full draw.
coaching_cues: Feel the same spot every time

drill.name: Eyes closed anchor
drill.description: With eyes closed, draw to your anchor point and hold for a few seconds, focusing on the tactile sensation of the string and hand on your face. Open eyes to check consistency.

entries.score_impact: Directly impacts aiming precision and shot repeatability, crucial for tight groups and high scores.
